one_line_takeaway: A reachability-based STL feasibility filter, placed between an LLM translator and a MILP mission planner, catches physically/temporally infeasible LLM-generated mission specifications that ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, and Llama 4 themselves failed to flag, and its decomposition scheme roughly halves MILP planning time — but this is a control/robotics reliability paper about dynamical feasibility verification, not an AI-security paper about adversarial threats.
key_finding: Scenario 1 (Table I): all three LLMs (ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Llama 4) judged the school-bus mission feasible or non-decisive when self-assessing (ChatGPT-4o: feasible; Claude 3.7 Sonnet and Llama 4: non-decisive), but the mission is actually infeasible because the school zone blocks access to goal g3 during the 8:30-9:00 AM mission window. The proposed SFF correctly flagged the mission as infeasible (x) in all three cases, catching a translation/reasoning flaw the LLMs themselves missed. Scenario 2: for a feasible mission with a 30-second horizon, MILP planning time dropped from 285.85s (original STL) to 40.67s (Level 1 decomposed STL) for the same mission, while trajectories from original vs. decomposed STL were both feasible but slightly different due to Level 1's conservativeness. When the STL was intentionally corrupted to be infeasible (an impossible conjunction '(g2 and r1) eventually'), the SFF correctly returned a null BRT and pinpointed exactly the modified subformula as the cause. Scenario 3 (Table II, n=100 Monte Carlo runs): average MILP computation time was 54.434 (original), 23.923 (Level 0), 25.163 (Level 1), 28.632 (Level 2) [units not stated in text, presumably seconds], i.e., roughly a 50% reduction from decomposition, with Level 0 fastest since it is an exact (non-conservative) rewriting.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a reachability-based 'STL feasibility filter' (SFF) that sits between an LLM translator and a mission planner in a human-autonomy teaming (HAT) pipeline. A human operator's natural-language command is translated by a (fine-tuned) LLM into a Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specification plus a dictionary of atomic predicates. The SFF then (1) decomposes the STL into simpler conjunctive subformulas using exact/conservative rewrite rules from Leahy et al. (levels 0/1/2), (2) computes a backward reachable tube (BRT) for each subformula via Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis (using the STL-robustness/Bellman-value-function correspondence of Chen et al. 2018) to check feasibility for the target dynamical system before any planning occurs, and (3) if any subformula is infeasible, forwards the specific infeasible subformula(s) back to the LLM to generate targeted, explanatory feedback to the human operator, rather than only a Boolean feasible/infeasible verdict. If all subformulas are feasible, a MILP-based mission planner (STL constraints encoded as mixed-integer linear constraints) generates the trajectory.
threat_model: Not applicable — this is not a security/adversarial paper. There is no attacker, adversary, or malicious input model. The 'safety/reliability' concern addressed is engineering reliability of LLM translation errors and physical/dynamical infeasibility of commands (e.g., an LLM failing to notice a time-window conflict), not adversarial manipulation, prompt injection, or any security threat.
limitations: The paper's own footnote 1 states the key limitation explicitly: the SFF verifies feasibility of each decomposed subformula independently but does not account for complex correlations between subformulas — two subformulas phi_A and phi_B may each individually yield a feasible (non-null) BRT while their conjunction phi_A AND phi_B is actually infeasible; this is deferred to future work via 'multiple reach-avoid' methods (cited as concurrent/future work, refs [21],[22]). The Conclusion also states as future work that a machine-learning-based BRT computation method will be explored to improve computational efficiency (current BRT computation via Hamilton-Jacobi reachability is not stated to scale well with state dimension, a known general limitation of that method class though not quantified in this paper). Evaluation is limited to three illustrative 2D-grid scenarios with double-integrator dynamics and a single quadrotor platform; no systematic robustness/failure-rate statistics are reported (Table I has n=1 per LLM per scenario), and units for Table II's computation-time numbers are not explicitly labeled in the paper.

datasets_used: No public dataset; three self-constructed illustrative scenarios (grid-based mission maps) used for numerical simulation and real-world experiments
quantitative_results: Scenario 1 (Table I): ChatGPT-4o self-check = feasible, SFF result = infeasible (correct); Claude 3.7 Sonnet self-check = non-decisive, SFF result = infeasible; Llama 4 self-check = non-decisive, SFF result = infeasible
Scenario 2: MILP mission-planning time for a 30s-horizon mission = 285.85s using the original (non-decomposed) STL vs. 40.67s using the Level 1 decomposed STL

Scenario 3 (Table II, 100 Monte Carlo simulations): average computation time = 54.434 (Original), 23.923 (Level 0), 25.163 (Level 1), 28.632 (Level 2); variance = 1.185 (Original), 0.212 (Level 0), 1.501 (Level 1), 2.633 (Level 2) — roughly 50% reduction in computation time from decomposition
baselines_compared: Original (non-decomposed) STL specification vs. Level 0/1/2 decomposed STL for MILP mission-planning computation time
Self-reported LLM feasibility assessments (ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Llama 4) vs. the proposed SFF's reachability-based feasibility verdict
